chemical_drugs: Lambda-cyhalothrin (pyrethroid) — use only where resistance is known to be low
other_medicines: Neem oil / azadirachtin formulations for repellence and larval development disruption
Pheromone traps for monitoring and mass-trapping

how_to_treat.description: Control focuses on early detection and targeting young larvae. Chemical insecticides are effective on small larvae but resistance management is critical. Integrated Pest Management (IPM) combines monitoring with biologicals (Bt, NPV), predators/parasitoids, and targeted chemical sprays when thresholds are reached.
step_by_step: Monitor fields weekly — inspect flowers and bolls; use pheromone traps to track male moth flights.
Determine action threshold (e.g., a set number of larvae per 100 plants or trap catch threshold).
If threshold exceeded, treat low-instar larvae with selective products: Bt or spinosad for minimal non-target impact.
If heavy infestation of larger larvae, use systemic/contact insecticides like chlorantraniliprole or emamectin benzoate following label guidance.
Rotate insecticide modes of action to reduce resistance (avoid repeated use of same group).
Release Trichogramma in hotspot areas and maintain refuge for natural predators (ladybirds, lacewings).
Record treatments and monitor for effectiveness — follow up if new outbreaks occur.

points1: Maintain field sanitation: remove and destroy crop residues and infested bolls after harvest to reduce overwintering populations.
Use pheromone traps for continuous monitoring and to time interventions accurately.
Plant early/late sowing strategies to avoid peak moth flights where locally recommended.
Adopt crop rotation and intercropping (e.g., non-host trap crops) to reduce pest build-up.
Encourage beneficial insects by growing insectary strips (e.g., buckwheat, sunn hemp) and avoiding broad-spectrum insecticides during flowering.
points2: Implement insecticide resistance management: rotate chemical classes and use biologicals as first line where possible.
Follow label rates and recommended withholding periods — avoid under-dosing (drives resistance) or overdosing (cost, residues).
Train field workers to recognize early damage symptoms and scout systematically (random sample of plants, record data).
Ensure irrigation and nutrition are balanced; stressed plants suffer greater damage and yield loss.
Coordinate area-wide management with neighboring farms for synchronous control (reduces reinvasion).

tips1: Integrate pheromone-based mating disruption in high-value or seed cotton blocks — reduces mating and long-term population growth.
Adopt real-time trap data logging (mobile apps) to predict peaks and plan interventions precisely.
tips2: Consider release of commercially available NPVs targeted to Helicoverpa for organic-compatible, high-specificity control during high-pressure years.
Use optical/AI scouting (drone or handheld plus model) to detect hotspots for spot-treatment rather than whole-field sprays, saving cost and preserving beneficials.

chemical_drugs: Difenoconazole or other triazoles (systemic) for curative action
Pyraclostrobin or azoxystrobin (QoI fungicides) used in rotation
other_medicines: Sulfur sprays (organic option) for mild infections
Copper-based fungicides (limited use due to phytotoxicity risk)
Kaput/biological fungicides containing Bacillus subtilis or other antagonist strains
Cultural sanitation (removal of fallen leaves and mummified fruit) — not a 'medicine' but critical

how_to_treat.description: Apple scab is primarily managed by protecting foliage during the wet, susceptible period. Preventive protectant sprays timed to leaf emergence and rain events are most effective; systemic fungicides help during established infections. Sanitation breaks the disease cycle.
step_by_step: Rake and remove all fallen leaves and mummified fruit in autumn or use leaf decomposition accelerants (e.g., urea sprays) where appropriate.
At bud break, apply a protectant fungicide such as mancozeb or captan according to local recommendations.
Monitor weather and re-apply protectants before/after major rain events during the vulnerable leaf and young fruit stage.
If scab lesions appear, apply a systemic or translaminar fungicide (e.g., difenoconazole) and alternate chemistry to avoid resistance.
Continue protective sprays through the critical period (typically until after fruit set and early season depending on region).
Remove and destroy severely infected shoots and fruit to reduce inoculum.

points1: Choose scab-resistant apple cultivars where possible (varieties labelled as scab-resistant dramatically reduce disease pressure).
Prune for good air circulation and light penetration to speed leaf drying and reduce favorable high-humidity microclimates.
Maintain good orchard sanitation: remove leaf litter and mummies to lower overwintering inoculum.
Implement a calendar of protectant sprays tied to local infection models (many extension services provide scab risk tools).
points2: Ensure balanced fertilization — excess nitrogen encourages dense, susceptible foliage.
Use ground cover or mulching to speed leaf decomposition and prevent spore splash-back from wet leaves.
Avoid overhead irrigation; if necessary, irrigate early in the day to allow leaves to dry quickly.
Record fungicide use and rotate modes of action to delay resistance to systemic fungicides.

tips1: Use disease forecasting models (local extension or online services) that combine leaf wetness and temperature to time sprays precisely and reduce unnecessary applications.
tips2: Introduce beneficial epiphytic microbes (commercial Bacillus-based products) as part of a spray program to establish a protective microflora on leaves.

common_name: Apple black rot (Botryosphaeria and related fungi)

chemical_drugs: Tebuconazole or other triazole fungicides for canker and fruit-rot control
Boscalid + pyraclostrobin mixes (as per label) for fruit protection
Captan as protectant spray during fruit development
other_medicines: Copper formulations for wound protection (limited and careful use)
Removal of cankered wood and mummified fruit (cultural control)
Biologicals containing Trichoderma spp. for pruning wound protection

how_to_treat.description: Black rot manifests as fruit rots and cankers. Management requires removal of infected tissues, protectant sprays during fruit maturation, and chemical treatments for severe outbreaks. Good pruning hygiene is essential to stop spread.
step_by_step: Identify and prune out cankered branches and dead wood — cut well into healthy wood and disinfect tools between cuts.
Collect and destroy mummified fruits from tree and ground immediately.
Apply protectant fungicides (captan) during fruit development and switch to systemic fungicides if active infections are observed.
Treat pruning wounds with appropriate protectants or biological wound protectants where available.
Sanitize tools and burn or bury infected debris to prevent reinfection.

points1: Inspect orchards regularly for early cankers and fruit lesions; remove infected material promptly.
Maintain tree vigor with balanced nutrition and irrigation to reduce susceptibility.
Avoid excessive canopy density; prune to improve airflow and light penetration.
points2: Sterilize pruning tools between trees in infected orchards to avoid mechanical spread.
Space trees appropriately and avoid planting into sites with poor drainage or heavy inoculum from prior infections.

tips1: After heavy rains or storms, do a focused inspection and remove any wounded or damaged tissue quickly — wounds are infection courts for black rot.
tips2: Combine biological wound protectants (Trichoderma) with minimal protective fungicide where label permits to reduce chemical reliance and support long-term disease suppression.

chemical_drugs: Myclobutanil or propiconazole (systemic triazoles) timed during the spring infection period
Protectants like mancozeb may reduce initial infections
other_medicines: Prune out heavily infected lashes and remove nearby alternate hosts (junipers/cupressaceae) if feasible
Resistant cultivars exist and are the most effective non-chemical measure

how_to_treat.description: The fungus requires both juniper (cedar) and apple/related hosts to complete its lifecycle. Management is through removing/isolating alternate hosts, protecting apple trees during spore-release windows, and using systemic fungicides at critical times.
step_by_step: Locate and identify nearby junipers or alternate hosts that may harbor the fungus during winter.
If possible, remove or trim junipers within a few hundred meters of orchards; alternatively, create buffer zones.
Apply protectant fungicides on apple trees at green-tip to petal-fall stages timed to predicted spore release from junipers.
If infections appear, apply a systemic fungicide and repeat according to label intervals.
Plant resistant apple varieties when establishing new blocks to reduce long-term risk.

points1: Map and manage alternate hosts in the landscape — even ornamental cedars in nearby gardens can sustain the disease.
Avoid planting susceptible apple cultivars adjacent to hedges of junipers.
points2: Time fungicide sprays to coincide with known spore-release periods (spring rains/wet spells).
Train staff to recognize early orange gelatinous telial horns on junipers in spring (indicator for imminent spore release).

tips1: Coordinate with neighbors/municipal authorities to manage ornamental junipers in the surrounding area if public plantings are a source of inoculum.
tips2: Use ‘trap’ monitoring — inspect junipers for telia formation and use that data to precisely time protective sprays on apples, reducing unnecessary applications.

chemical_drugs: Alpha-cypermethrin or lambda-cyhalothrin for heavy, rapid knockdown (careful — non-selective)
other_medicines: Nomuraea rileyi (entomopathogenic fungus) and NPV biopesticides where available
Encourage natural enemies (parasitoid wasps, birds) and use light/pheromone traps for monitoring

how_to_treat.description: Armyworms can cause rapid defoliation. Early detection and quick response are essential — target small larvae with selective bioinsecticides; reserve broad-spectrum chemicals for large outbreaks and only where necessary to protect beneficials.
step_by_step: Scout fields daily when moth flights are detected; focus on margins and young plants.
If small larvae dominate, apply Bt or spinosad for good control with minimal beneficial impact.
For heavy infestations of larger larvae, use chlorantraniliprole or emamectin benzoate following label rates.
Spot-spray hotspots rather than blanket-spraying entire fields where practical.
After control, maintain monitoring for late-emerging cohorts and treat if thresholds are again exceeded.

points1: Maintain weed-free field margins — many armyworm species breed on grasses and weeds which act as refuges.
Use light traps or pheromone traps to monitor adult moth flights and predict larval outbreaks.
points2: Encourage and conserve natural enemies by avoiding untargeted pyrethroid use during peak predator activity periods.
Stagger plantings or use synchronous planting at landscape scale to reduce continuous availability of preferred hosts.

tips1: Use predictive models tied to trap catches and degree-day accumulation to anticipate outbreaks and preemptively treat only where necessary.
tips2: In high-value crops, integrate banker plants or augmentative releases of parasitoids (e.g., Trichogramma) timed to egg-laying peaks for sustained suppression.

common_name: Bacterial blight of rice (Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae)

chemical_drugs: No curative chemical 'antibiotic' widely recommended for field use in many countries; copper-based bactericides (e.g., copper hydroxide) are used as protectants but have limited efficacy.
Certain systemic plant defense inducers (e.g., acibenzolar-S-methyl) used in some programs to boost resistance.
other_medicines: Resistant varieties (the single most effective measure)
Seed treatments with antagonistic bacteria/fungi (Bacillus spp.) and good seed health management
Implementing cultural controls — optimal spacing, balanced fertilization

how_to_treat.description: Because bacterial blight is systemic and difficult to cure once established, management focuses on prevention: using resistant varieties, clean seed, and cultural measures. Chemical bactericides give only partial control and should be used as part of an integrated program.
step_by_step: Select and plant rice cultivars with known resistance genes (locally recommended resistant varieties).
Use certified disease-free seed and treat seed where appropriate.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which favors severe epidemics — follow recommended N regimes and timing.
Implement proper water management (avoid prolonged leaf wetness and excessive standing water during vulnerable stages if feasible).
If local guidelines recommend copper sprays or SAR inducers, apply them as protectants early in the disease window; consult local extension for approved products and timing.
Rogue out heavily infected plants in nurseries to limit spread.

points1: Use resistant cultivars as the primary strategy — stack resistance genes where possible for durable control.
Strictly practice seed health management: certified seed, hot-water seed treatments if recommended locally, and avoid transplanting infected seedlings.
points2: Manage nitrogen carefully — split applications and avoid excessive pre-heading N to reduce susceptibility.
Maintain field hygiene and minimize equipment movement between infected and clean fields without sanitation.

tips1: Collaborate with local breeding programs to adopt new resistant lines adapted to your area — combining resistance genes reduces risk of breakdown.
tips2: Use pathogen monitoring (strain typing where available) to know which resistance genes are effective locally and adapt variety choice accordingly.

chemical_drugs: Protectant fungicides such as mancozeb or chlorothalonil for leaf-spot control
Systemic fungicides (triazoles like tebuconazole) in severe cases
other_medicines: Seed treatment with Trichoderma or Pseudomonas antagonist strains
Crop rotation and removal of volunteer beans and residue
Use of resistant/tolerant cultivars where available

how_to_treat.description: Angular leaf spot spreads rapidly under warm, humid conditions. Timely protectant sprays and good sanitation minimize yield loss. Seed and residue management reduce initial inoculum.
step_by_step: Use disease-free seed and treat seed with biologicals or recommended fungicide seed dressings.
Apply protectant fungicides at first sign of disease or when weather favors disease (frequent rains/high humidity).
Use systemic fungicides if disease is widespread, alternating modes of action to avoid resistance.
Remove infected crop residue and avoid planting beans in the same field consecutively.
Maintain adequate plant spacing for airflow and reduce canopy humidity.

points1: Rotate with non-host crops (e.g., cereals) for at least one season to reduce pathogen carryover.
Use raised beds and adequate spacing to improve drainage and reduce leaf wetness duration.
points2: Avoid late-season dense foliage through pruning or thinning where applicable to reduce humid microclimates.
Sanitize seed sources and inspect seed lots for symptoms before planting.

tips1: Adopt on-farm seed multiplication under strict sanitation to ensure an ongoing supply of clean seed for subsequent seasons.
tips2: Combine foliar biological fungicides with protectants on a rotation schedule to maintain efficacy and support beneficial phyllosphere microbes.

chemical_drugs: Protectant fungicides such as chlorothalonil or mancozeb
Systemic fungicides (e.g., triazoles) for severe infections
other_medicines: Cultural measures: sanitation and crop rotation

how_to_treat.description: Rust on beans shows as pustules and can reduce photosynthetic area. Control relies on resistant cultivars and timely protectant fungicides; systemic fungicides can limit spread in severe outbreaks.
step_by_step: Plant resistant/tolerant cultivars when possible.
Apply protectant fungicides before conditions that favor rust (damp, cool-moderate weather).
If pustules are widespread, apply systemic fungicides and follow up with protectants.
Remove heavily infected plants to reduce spore load if practical in small plantings.

points1: Avoid overhead irrigation late in the day and schedule watering early to allow leaves to dry.
Rotate with non-host crops to reduce pathogen build-up in soil and residue.
points2: Avoid overcrowding and maintain good ventilation in beds/rows.
Monitor for early pustules; early intervention reduces need for repeated sprays.

tips1: Implement varietal blends (mixing resistant and tolerant lines) to reduce epidemic development at smallholder scale.
tips2: Use sentinel plots at field margins to give early warning of rust build-up in the area and time sprays regionally.

common_name: Bell pepper bacterial spot (Xanthomonas euvesicatoria / X. campestris)

chemical_drugs: Copper-based bactericides (copper hydroxide or copper oxychloride) — protectant effect
Combination copper + mancozeb products in some regions for enhanced protection
other_medicines: Use of disease-free transplants and seed treatments
Biological antagonists (Bacillus subtilis products) for suppression

how_to_treat.description: Bacterial spot is best managed preventively. Copper sprays help reduce disease spread; however, bacteria may develop copper tolerance. Integrated measures — clean seed, resistant varieties, and sanitation — are essential.
step_by_step: Grow or buy certified disease-free transplants; inspect seedlings before transplanting.
Apply copper sprays as protectants at the first sign of wet weather or when disease is known in the area.
Alternate copper with biological products to reduce selection for copper-tolerant strains.
Remove severely infected plants and avoid working in the field when foliage is wet to minimize mechanical spread.

points1: Sanitize tools and hands between blocks to reduce mechanical transfer of bacteria.
Avoid overhead irrigation or irrigate early so foliage dries quickly.
points2: Rogue infected transplants immediately; do not move suspect plants between greenhouses.
Use mulch or plastic film to reduce soil splash onto lower leaves which can carry bacteria.

tips1: Implement periodic pathogen monitoring for copper tolerance; if present, shift strategy to stronger emphasis on biologicals and cultural practices.
tips2: Use grafting onto tolerant rootstocks (where available) for high-value pepper production to reduce foliar disease impact and increase yield stability.

common_name: Brown spot (general name — context-dependent; commonly rice/broadleaf brown spots)

chemical_drugs: For rice brown spot: copper oxychloride or mancozeb as protectants
Triazole fungicides (tebuconazole) where label permits for systemic control
other_medicines: Seed treatments with Trichoderma or other antagonists

how_to_treat.description: Brown spot diseases vary by host; general approach is protectant fungicides, seed treatments, and cultural measures to improve plant vigor. Critical to identify the host crop and pathogen for precise selection of chemical controls.
step_by_step: Confirm pathogen identity (if possible) — brown spot on rice vs. broadleaf differ in optimal control.
Use disease-free seed and apply recommended seed treatments.
Apply protectant fungicides during periods of high humidity or when symptoms first appear.
Adjust fertilization (avoid potassium/micronutrient deficiencies) and improve drainage where waterlogging contributes to disease.

points1: Maintain healthy, vigorous plants through balanced nutrient management; stressed plants show worse brown spot severity.
Use crop rotation to reduce pathogen carryover in many cases.
points2: Improve field drainage and reduce prolonged leaf wetness; avoid dense canopies that trap humidity.
Sanitize seed and debris to lower primary inoculum for subsequent seasons.

tips1: Map nutrient status periodically (leaf tissue testing) to identify and correct deficiencies that predispose to brown spot outbreaks.
tips2: Use integrated seed systems (on-farm certified seed protocol) to maintain clean seed stock and reduce brown spot introduction.

chemical_drugs: Triadimefon or trifloxystrobin (as per local label and resistance guidelines)
other_medicines: Potassium bicarbonate sprays (organic option, contact effect)
Biologicals containing Bacillus subtilis for suppression
Pruning and canopy management to reduce humidity

how_to_treat.description: Powdery mildew thrives in warm, dry days with cool nights and high humidity. Management emphasizes early fungicide sprays at first sign or during susceptible growth stages plus cultural measures to improve airflow and reduce inoculum.
step_by_step: Scout for the white powdery mycelium on young leaves, shoots and fruitlets during spring-summer.
Apply a protectant sulfur spray at first detection and repeat at label intervals while conditions remain favorable.
If lesions are established, use a systemic fungicide (triazole) for curative action; rotate chemistry to avoid resistance.
Prune to open canopy, remove infected shoots and destroy prunings away from orchard.
Maintain good nutrition, avoiding excessive nitrogen that encourages succulent growth favored by mildew.

points1: Prune trees to improve light penetration and air movement, reducing favorable microclimates for powdery mildew.
Avoid excessive high-nitrogen fertilizers; apply balanced nutrition to reduce overly succulent growth.
points2: Use sulfur or biological protectants during repeated-risk periods and rotate with systemic fungicides to prevent resistance.
Remove and destroy heavily infected debris and shoots to lower overwintering inoculum.

tips1: Apply anti-sporulant biologicals (Bacillus-based) as part of an alternating spray program to support long-term suppression and protect beneficials.
tips2: Monitor microclimate (leaf wetness/relative humidity) inside canopy with low-cost sensors to time preventative sprays precisely and reduce unnecessary treatments.

common_name: Chilli damping-off (soilborne fungi: Pythium, Rhizoctonia, Fusarium, etc.)

chemical_drugs: Seed treatments with metalaxyl-M (for Pythium) or thiram/carboxin (as per local approvals)
Soil drenches with azoxystrobin + difenoconazole or prochloraz in nursery management where permitted
other_medicines: Use of Trichoderma-based biocontrols in soil or as seed treatment
Sterile or pasteurized potting mix, good nursery hygiene
Hot-water treatment of seed (where appropriate) and certified pathogen-free seed

how_to_treat.description: Damping-off affects seedlings and is often catastrophic in nurseries. Prevention (clean media, seed treatment, and sanitation) is the cornerstone. Chemical drenches can suppress outbreaks but must be applied early.
step_by_step: Use certified disease-free seed and treat seed with recommended fungicidal/bio seed treatments before sowing.
Prepare sterile or well-drained growing media; avoid re-using contaminated soil.
Ensure benches/trays have excellent drainage; avoid overwatering and water from below where possible.
If damping-off appears, remove and destroy affected trays, apply appropriate soil drench in adjacent trays and adjust watering/nursery sanitation.
Introduce Trichoderma or other antagonists in substrate as long-term preventive measure.

points1: Maintain nursery hygiene: sanitize trays, tools, benches and use clean water sources for irrigation.
Avoid overcrowding of seedlings; provide ventilation and gentle air movement to reduce humidity.
points2: Use properly composted/sterile potting mixes and avoid high organic loads that favor pathogenic oomycetes.
Rotate substrate sources and avoid reusing old potting soil without pasteurization or composting.

tips1: Incorporate biological consortia (Trichoderma + Bacillus spp.) into potting mixes as a long-term suppressive approach rather than relying solely on chemical drenches.
tips2: Monitor nursery temperature and moisture closely; automated bottom-watering with moisture sensors reduces overwatering risks that fuel damping-off.

chemical_drugs: Protectant fungicides: mancozeb or chlorothalonil during pre-harvest fruit development
Systemic fungicides: carbendazim (where allowed) or azoxystrobin/thiophanate-methyl in rotation for control of active infections
other_medicines: Hot-water seed treatment and seed certification to reduce seedborne inoculum
Biologicals such as Bacillus subtilis or Trichoderma formulations for suppressive spray programs
Post-harvest sanitation and careful handling to reduce fruit rots

how_to_treat.description: Anthracnose infects fruit and causes significant post-harvest losses. Managing inoculum from seed, residue, and infected fruit plus timely protectant sprays during wet seasons is essential. Post-harvest handling reduces rot development.
step_by_step: Use certified disease-free seed or treat seed with hot water / appropriate seed treatment to reduce seedborne Colletotrichum.
Apply protectant fungicides during flowering and fruit set and continue at intervals during rainy periods.
If fruit infections appear, use systemic fungicides according to label and rotate modes of action.
Practice strict field sanitation: remove mummified fruit, avoid fruit-to-fruit contact, and ensure good field drainage.
Handle harvested fruit carefully, cool quickly and avoid bruising which predisposes to post-harvest rot.

points1: Avoid overhead irrigation during fruit ripening; if necessary irrigate early so fruit dries quickly.
Plant in well-drained sites and maintain good plant spacing for air flow.
points2: Sanitize harvest containers and transport to prevent cross-contamination of healthy fruit.
Rotate with non-host crops and remove volunteers to lower field inoculum.

tips1: Adopt integrated pre- and post-harvest programs: field sprays combined with rapid post-harvest cooling to cut losses from anthracnose.
tips2: Use cultivar selection — choose capsicum varieties with known field tolerance to Colletotrichum where available.

other_medicines: Preventive foliar nutrition and biostimulants to maintain plant vigor
Routine monitoring and use of low-impact biologicals for phyllosphere protection

how_to_treat.description: No treatment required. Emphasis should be on maintaining plant health to prevent disease introduction and on regular scouting to detect early problems.
step_by_step: Maintain recommended fertilization and irrigation schedules.
Scout regularly for pests and diseases; act promptly on early signs.
Use cover crops, organic matter and beneficial microbes to maintain soil health.
Sanitize tools and limit movement from other fields to prevent pathogen introduction.

points1: Adopt integrated pest and disease management practices even when plants are healthy to prolong disease-free status.
Use mulches and drip irrigation to reduce leaf wetness and soil splash.
points2: Keep records of crop performance, inputs and pest/disease observations to spot trends early.
Implement border monitoring (sentinel rows) to detect incoming threats from nearby fields.

tips1: Use weekly low-dose biological foliar sprays (Bacillus/Beneficial consortia) as a protective microbiome reinforcement strategy.
tips2: Apply precision nutrition (foliar micro-nutrients based on tissue tests) to keep plants resilient against episodic stress that can trigger disease.

common_name: Chilli leaf curl (Tomato/Chilli leaf curl virus complex transmitted by whitefly)

chemical_drugs: No direct antiviral chemical; control focuses on vector control using systemic/insecticidal options like imidacloprid (seedling treatment) and insect growth regulators or neonicotinoids where allowed
Use of contact insecticides (pyrethroids) may reduce whitefly briefly but monitor resistance
other_medicines: Remove and destroy infected plants to reduce virus source
Use of reflective mulches and yellow sticky traps to reduce whitefly landing
Resistant/tolerant varieties and grafting onto tolerant rootstocks where available

how_to_treat.description: Leaf curl is viral — incurable once systemically infected. Management targets prevention: vector (whitefly) suppression, use of clean transplants, and removal of infected plants to limit virus spread.
step_by_step: Use virus-free transplants and install physical barriers (nets) in nurseries where feasible.
Treat seedlings with systemic insecticide seedling treatments per label to protect them from early whitefly attack.
Deploy yellow sticky traps and reflective mulches to deter and monitor whitefly populations.
Rogue out infected plants immediately and destroy them (do not compost) to remove virus reservoirs.
Coordinate whitefly management with surrounding growers to lower regional vector pressure.

points1: Maintain strict nursery hygiene and isolate new transplants until inspected for symptoms.
Avoid planting during peak whitefly seasons when possible; consider staggered planting to avoid vulnerable windows.
points2: Encourage natural enemies of whitefly (ladybirds, lacewings) by planting insectary strips and reducing broad-spectrum insecticide use.
Implement physical exclusion (fine-mesh netting) for high-value protected crops.

tips1: Use area-wide integrated whitefly management combining reflective mulches, sticky traps, biological control and targeted insecticide rotations to keep vector pressure low.
tips2: Apply RNAi- or cross-protection-based technologies only where proven and permitted (research-stage in many regions); consult local authorities and experts before use.

common_name: Chilli leaf spot (various pathogens including Alternaria, Cercospora, Colletotrichum)

chemical_drugs: Protectants: mancozeb or chlorothalonil during wet seasons
Systemics: azoxystrobin or tebuconazole in rotation for active outbreaks
other_medicines: Use of clean seed and seed treatments to reduce seedborne pathogens

how_to_treat.description: Leaf spot control uses protectant fungicides and cultural measures to reduce humidity and inoculum. Identify the causal genus if possible to pick the most effective systemic fungicide.
step_by_step: Inspect plants early in the season for small spots; implement protectant sprays when rainy/humid conditions begin.
Rotate systemic fungicides with different modes of action to avoid resistance.
Remove heavily infected lower leaves and avoid practices that splash soil onto foliage.
Ensure good fertility and watering practices to reduce plant stress.

points1: Do not let plant canopies become overly dense; prune or thin to promote airflow.
Use drip irrigation to avoid leaf wetting.
points2: Avoid late-season irrigation that keeps leaves wet overnight and increases disease spread.
Sanitize stakes, trellises and tools between seasons to reduce carryover.

tips1: Use integrated spray programs combining low-toxicity biologicals and infrequent systemic applications to preserve efficacy and reduce residues.
tips2: Adopt cultivar selection and local adaptation trials to find varieties with better leaf-spot tolerance under your microclimate.

common_name: Chilli mottle (could indicate viral or nutrient disorder — e.g., mosaic/mottle viruses)

chemical_drugs: No chemical cure for viral causes; manage vectors (aphids, whiteflies) with registered insecticides if necessary
other_medicines: Remove and destroy infected plants (if viral) to prevent spread
Ensure balanced micronutrient regime to rule out nutritional mottle (soil test guided)

how_to_treat.description: Mottle symptoms can arise from viruses (mosaic/mottle viruses) or from nutrient imbalances. Correct diagnosis is critical. Viral infections are managed by vector control and plant removal; nutrient causes are corrected with targeted fertilization.
step_by_step: Diagnose cause: send samples to extension lab or compare pattern — mosaic/vein clearing suggests virus; uniform chlorosis/mottling suggests nutrient deficiency.
If viral, rogue infected plants, control vectors with integrated measures (biocontrols, selective insecticides) and use virus-free seed/transplants.
If nutritional, perform soil and tissue tests and apply targeted fertilization (e.g., magnesium, manganese, zinc) according to recommendations.
Implement crop hygiene and avoid moving sap-contaminated tools between plants.

points1: Use certified virus-free seed and resistant varieties where available.
Monitor and control common vectors using cultural (reflective mulches, intercropping) and biological measures.
points2: Maintain good soil fertility and pH to prevent nutrient imbalances that mimic disease symptoms.
Keep records of symptoms and management actions to identify recurrent problems early.

tips1: If viral, implement an area-wide virus management plan with neighboring farms because viruses and vectors move across properties rapidly.
tips2: Use rapid on-farm diagnostic kits or send samples to labs for precise virus identification when mottle appears in multiple fields to guide long-term strategy.

common_name: Chilli whitefly (Bemisia tabaci and related species)

chemical_drugs: Neonicotinoids (e.g., imidacloprid) as seedling/young-plant protection where permitted
Insect growth regulators (pyriproxyfen) and selective insecticides like spiromesifen/ spirotetramat for sap-feeders
Avoid sole reliance on pyrethroids due to resistance and non-target effects
other_medicines: Encarsia formosa and other parasitoids (biological control) in protected or greenhouse production
Yellow sticky traps for monitoring and mass-trapping

how_to_treat.description: Whitefly populations can explode and spread viruses (leaf curl, mosaic). IPM focusing on monitoring, biologicals, cultural deterrents and targeted insecticide rotations is most effective. Preserve natural enemies where possible.
step_by_step: Monitor using yellow sticky traps and regular leaf inspections for nymphs on undersides of leaves.
If low levels, augment natural enemies and apply selective insecticides (spiromesifen, spirotetramat) timed to nymphal stages for best effect.
Use seedling treatments (systemic) for high-value transplants but rotate actives to delay resistance.
Implement physical deterrents: reflective mulch, insect-proof netting in nurseries, and removal of alternate host weeds.
Record treatments and evaluate whitefly population response to adjust strategy.

points1: Maintain weed-free surroundings; many weeds harbor whiteflies and act as refuges.
Establish insectary strips to attract and keep natural enemies in the landscape.
points2: Avoid broad-spectrum insecticide sprays during bloom or when beneficials are active; target sprays at peak nymphal vulnerability.
Coordinate regional whitefly management with neighboring farms to reduce reinfestation.

tips1: Use biological control augmentations (Encarsia, Eretmocerus) combined with banker plants in protected culture for sustainable suppression.
tips2: Apply precision release of parasitoids tied to trap counts and use sticky-trap networks with mobile logging to guide release timing.

common_name: Chilli yellowing (non-specific; could be nutrient deficiency, viral disease, or physiological stress)

chemical_drugs: If vector-borne virus suspected, control vectors with appropriate insecticides (see whitefly/aphid measures)
No chemical cures for viral causes; focus on vector and cultural management
other_medicines: Corrective nutrient applications (nitrogen, magnesium, iron, zinc) based on soil/tissue tests
Improve irrigation and drainage to avoid waterlogging or drought stress
